title: Methods: Generating Biomarker Data with Specified AUC
text: Hanley JA, McNeil BJ (1982). meaning use area receiver operating characteristic (ROC) curve. Radiology, 143(1), 29–36. DeLong ER, DeLong DM, Clarke-Pearson DL (1988). Comparing areas two correlated receiver operating characteristic curves. Biometrics, 44(3), 837–845.

title: aucmat: Matrix-First ROC Analysis
text: Receiver operating characteristic (ROC) analysis dominant framework evaluating continuous biomarkers binary outcomes diagnostic medicine, prognostic modeling, molecular screening (Hanley McNeil 1982; DeLong et al. 1988; Pepe 2004). area ROC curve (AUC) summarizes probability randomly chosen positive subject higher biomarker value randomly chosen negative subject. interpretability – 0.5 chance, 1.0 perfect separation – made default metric omics-scale biomarker discovery (Robin et al. 2011). Modern molecular profiling technologies routinely measure thousands features hundred subjects. proteomics panel may contain 5,000 proteins; metabolomics screen 2,000 metabolites; transcriptomics experiment 20,000 genes. core statistical task – “biomarkers discriminate cases controls?” – requires computing testing AUC feature, adjusting multiplicity, comparing top candidates, assessing stability rankings sampling variability. Despite decades methodological development, widely-used R package integrates steps single, reproducible workflow. existing R ecosystem provides excellent point solutions. pROC (Robin et al. 2011) gold standard single-biomarker ROC inference: computes DeLong confidence intervals using Sun & Xu (2014) O(N log N) algorithm (Sun Xu 2014), tests paired unpaired AUC differences via roc.test(), handles partial AUC bootstrap inference, provides binormal density-smoothed ROC curves, computes covariance paired AUCs via cov(), supports multiclass AUC via Hand & Till (Hand Till 2001). However, pROC operates one biomarker time – screening matrix 10,000 features requires explicit per-column loop. ROCR (Sing et al. 2005) offers flexible visualization 28 performance measures cross-validation averaging provides statistical inference (confidence intervals, hypothesis tests AUC differences). precrec (Saito Rehmsmeier 2017) adds precision-recall curves fast C++-based AUC computation, confidence bands multiple test sets functions partial AUC (part(), pauc()) AUC confidence intervals (auc_ci()), provides DeLong inference, multiplicity adjustment, paired comparison tests. colAUC caTools (Tuszynski Dietze 2024) computes column-wise AUCs efficiently via Wilcoxon rank-sum test supports multiclass AUC via column means – provides inference whatsoever (standard errors, confidence intervals, p-values). dtComb (Yerlitas et al. 2025) provides 142 methods combining two diagnostic markers composite score, 30+ cutoff optimization methods, resampling, caret integration – limited exactly two biomarkers perform matrix-wide screening. cancerclass (Budczies et al. 2024) builds validated classifiers high-dimensional molecular data using nearest-centroid prediction multiple random validation, offer DeLong-based screening bootstrap stability analysis. aucmat fills gap. design philosophy matrix-first: primary function aucmat(X, y) accepts numeric matrix \mathbf{X} \\mathbb{R}^{n \times p} binary outcome vector \mathbf{y} \\{0,1\}^n, returns complete screening table point estimates, standard errors, confidence intervals, raw multiplicity-adjusted p-values, diagnostic metadata every biomarker single call. Downstream functions comparison, stability, cross-validation, panel construction, power analysis consume S3 object, preserving outcome encoding inferential settings throughout.

title: aucmat: Matrix-First ROC Analysis
text: biomarker X binary outcome Y \\{0,1\}, AUC equals Mann-Whitney U probability: \text{AUC} = P(X_i > X_j \mid Y_i = 1, Y_j = 0) (Hanley McNeil 1982). ROC software reports \max(\text{AUC}, 1 - \text{AUC}), silently reversing biomarker force reported value 0.5. aucmat deliberately preserves observed direction, reporting three values per biomarker: \text{AUC}_{\text{raw}} (Mann-Whitney estimate original numeric scale, may 0.5), \text{AUC}_{\text{strength}} = 0.5 + |\text{AUC}_{\text{raw}} - 0.5| (ranking), effect_direction (higher_in_positive lower_in_positive). distinction matters biologically: tumor suppressor gene \text{AUC}_{\text{raw}} = 0.15 discriminates just strongly oncogene \text{AUC}_{\text{raw}} = 0.85, opposite biological interpretation.

title: aucmat: Matrix-First ROC Analysis
text: DeLong variance estimator (DeLong et al. 1988; Sun Xu 2014) uses placement values V_{10}(X_i) = n_0^{-1} \sum_{j:Y_j=0} [\mathbf{1}(X_j < X_i) + 0.5 \cdot \mathbf{1}(X_j = X_i)] positive subjects V_{01}(X_j) = n_1^{-1} \sum_{:Y_i=1} [\mathbf{1}(X_i > X_j) + 0.5 \cdot \mathbf{1}(X_i = X_j)] negative subjects. Let S_{10} = (n_1 - 1)^{-1} \sum_{\\mathcal{}_1} (V_{10}(X_i) - \bar{V}_{10})^2 S_{01} = (n_0 - 1)^{-1} \sum_{j \\mathcal{}_0} (V_{01}(X_j) - \bar{V}_{01})^2 sample variances placement values. DeLong variance estimator \widehat{\text{Var}}(\widehat{\text{AUC}}) = S_{10}/n_1 + S_{01}/n_0. p biomarkers common subjects, p \times p joint covariance matrix \widehat{\mathbf{\Sigma}} = n_1^{-1}\widehat{\text{Cov}}(\mathbf{V}_{10}) + n_0^{-1}\widehat{\text{Cov}}(\mathbf{V}_{01}) supports omnibus Wald test H_0: \text{AUC}_1 = \cdots = \text{AUC}_p. Let \mathbf{C} (p-1) \times p contrast matrix (default: comparing biomarkers 2,\ldots,p biomarker 1). Wald statistic W = (\mathbf{C}\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}})^\top (\mathbf{C}\widehat{\mathbf{\Sigma}}\mathbf{C}^\top)^{-1} (\mathbf{C}\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}) \sim \chi^2_{r} uses effective rank r contrast covariance matrix, determined via eigenvalue tolerance. generalized inverse used stable subspace ; rank-zero numerically unstable cases return status = "non_testable_singular". p = 2, W equals squared paired-DeLong z-statistic within numerical tolerance, serving internal consistency check.

title: aucmat: Matrix-First ROC Analysis
text: Screening p biomarkers produces p hypothesis tests. aucmat applies Benjamini-Hochberg (Benjamini Hochberg 1995), Holm, Bonferroni correction via stats::p.adjust(), reporting adjusted p-values q-values. equivalence (TOST) (Schuirmann 1987), reported p_{\text{equiv}} = \max(p_{\text{lower}}, p_{\text{upper}}) multiplicity adjustment applies combined value. Bootstrap rank-stability analysis (auc_stability()) resamples data B times replacement (stratified outcome), recomputes full screening replicate, reports median/IQR rank distributions, top-k selection probabilities, pairwise co-selection frequencies – quantifying reproducible ranking sampling variability (Efron Tibshirani 1994).

title: aucmat: Matrix-First ROC Analysis
text: Standard AUC assumes continuous biomarkers following shifted normal distribution per class. assumption breaks zero-inflated data (scRNA-seq, microbiome, detection-limited assays) 60%+ values exactly zero. standard model, zeros need arise tail normal distribution zero – biologically implausible. Hurdle-AUC uses two-stage model: Stage 1 – Zero hurdle: P(X_j = 0 \mid Y) = \text{logistic}(\beta_0 + \beta_1 Y) models binary expression decision via logistic regression. Stage 2 – Magnitude: Standard AUC non-zero values captures magnitude discrimination among expressed observations. composite score S_i observation : S_i = \hat{p}_i X_{ij} = 0, S_i = 0.5 \cdot \hat{p}_i + 0.5 \cdot \tilde{x}_{ij} X_{ij} > 0, \hat{p}_i predicted probability non-zero Stage 1, \tilde{x}_{ij} normalized biomarker value. Simulation confirms recovery standard AUC \approx 0.53 Hurdle-AUC \approx 0.90 zero-inflated biomarkers acknowledging “expression /” “expression level ” separate biological processes.

title: aucmat: Matrix-First ROC Analysis
text: Screening ranks biomarkers individually; combining several one score distinct problem, addressed fit_auc_panel(). Four six methods ridge, lasso, elastic-net, unpenalized logistic regression centred scaled predictors, penalized variants fit via glmnet (Friedman et al. 2010). fifth, su_liu, closed-form linear combination maximizes AUC common-covariance multivariate normal model (Su Liu 1993): class-conditional means \boldsymbol{\mu}_1, \boldsymbol{\mu}_0 pooled within-class covariance \boldsymbol{\Sigma}, AUC-maximizing direction \mathbf{w} = \boldsymbol{\Sigma}^{-1} (\boldsymbol{\mu}_1 - \boldsymbol{\mu}_0) – direction Fisher’s linear discriminant, natural counterpart binormal model already used aucmat’s simulation engine. \boldsymbol{\Sigma} frequently ill-conditioned biomarkers collinear p approaches n, pooled covariance shrunk toward diagonal, \boldsymbol{\Sigma}_\lambda = (1-\lambda)\boldsymbol{\Sigma} + \lambda\,\mathrm{diag}(\boldsymbol{\Sigma}) user-set \lambda \[0,1], reciprocal-condition-number guard fails informatively rather returning unstable solution. sixth, unweighted, sums direction-aligned, standardized biomarkers equal weight fits parameters – assumption-light baseline judging whether fitted methods earn added complexity. six methods share one nested cross-validation procedure reported --fold AUC: centering, scaling, (penalized methods) penalty selection re-derived outer fold’s training portion alone, including final full-data model’s internal penalty search, nothing derived held-fold ever contributes model evaluated . compare_panel_auc() reuses DeLong bootstrap paired-comparison engine compare_auc() test panel’s honest cross-validated score individual biomarker used build , subjects, plot_roc_panel() plots corresponding ROC curves together.

title: aucmat: Matrix-First ROC Analysis
text: equal-variance binormal model, X_k \mid Y \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu_{kY}, \sigma^2) \delta_k = (\mu_{1k} - \mu_{0k})/\sigma = \sqrt{2} \cdot \Phi^{-1}(\text{AUC}_k). p biomarkers correlation matrix \mathbf{R}, class-conditional distribution \mathbf{X} \mid Y \sim \mathcal{N}_p(\boldsymbol{\mu}_Y, \mathbf{R}), \boldsymbol{\mu}_0 \boldsymbol{\mu}_1 centred n-weighted grand mean zero. simulate_auc_matrix() generates data model four parametrized correlation structures (user, exchangeable, AR(1), block) feasibility diagnostics optional nearest positive-definite projection. simulate_auc_copula() uses two-phase approach: Phase 1 applies Gaussian copula (rank-transform uniform, probit normal scores) preserves target correlation structure; Phase 2 applies iterative mean perturbation decaying step size fine-tune biomarker’s AUC. separates correlation control (copula) discriminative signal (perturbation), avoiding AUC-correlation tradeoff inherent sequential binormal methods. simulate_hurdle_auc() implements two-stage hurdle model: Bernoulli zeros class-specific rates, log-normal magnitudes expressed values binormal-calibrated mean separation, clipped [0, \infty). validate_simulation() repeats specification across independent draws reports bias, RMSE, Monte Carlo standard error, target-interval hit rates AUCs pairwise correlations – single draw never evidence simulator calibrated.

title: aucmat: Matrix-First ROC Analysis
text: aucmat implemented pure R (~6,900 lines across 37 source files) minimized dependencies. primary function aucmat() performs six steps sequence: (1) input validation (matrix dimensions, column names, outcome levels, infinite values); (2) outcome normalization via .normalize_binary_y() (accepting logical, numeric 0/1, factor, character outcomes); (3) matrix-wide AUC computation via Mann-Whitney rank formulation; (4) DeLong bootstrap inference via .apply_inference() configurable alternative hypothesis (two.sided, greater, less); (5) multiplicity adjustment via .adjust_pvalues(); (6) result assembly ranking discrimination strength feature-level warnings small class counts high missingness. primary functions return S3 objects print(), summary(), plot(), (appropriate) .data.frame() subset() methods. package stores outcome encoding, positive-class designation, sample masks returned object, eliminating need downstream functions re-infer factor direction re-derive class counts. Structured error classes (inheriting error) allow programmatic handling common failures: aucmat_invalid_outcome, aucmat_invalid_correlation, aucmat_infeasible_targets, aucmat_insufficient_sample, aucmat_resampling_failure. RNG-safety mechanism (with_seed()) saves global .Random.seed, executes computation, restores previous state exit, including .Random.seed previously absent error occurs. package imports pROC (ROC curve objects smoothed ROC), mvtnorm (multivariate normal generation), ggplot2 (visualization), rlang (tidy evaluation). Optional Suggests include GGally (pairs plots simulated data), glmnet (penalized panel models), matrixStats (fast column-wise ranks), knitr rmarkdown (vignette building), ggrepel (non-overlapping plot labels). complete source, documentation, four vignettes available https://vanhungtran.github.io/aucmat.

code: library(aucmat)  sim <- simulate_auc_matrix(   n = 500, prevalence = 0.3,   target_aucs = c(0.9, 0.8, 0.7, 0.65),   correlation = 0.3, structure = "exchangeable" ) X <- as.matrix(sim$data[, 1:4]) y <- sim$data$truth  fit <- aucmat(X, y, ci = "delong", adjust = "BH") print(fit) summary(fit) plot_auc_rank(fit, n_label = 4)

code: # Two-sided superiority (default: H0: delta = 0) compare_auc(fit, X, y, reference = "X1")  # Non-inferiority: H0: AUC_a <= AUC_b - margin compare_auc(fit, X, y, reference = "X1",   hypothesis = "noninferiority", margin = 0.05)  # Equivalence (TOST): H0: |AUC_a - AUC_b| >= margin compare_auc(fit, X, y, reference = "X1",   hypothesis = "equivalence", margin = 0.15, adjust = "BH")  # Omnibus Wald test: H0: AUC_1 = AUC_2 = ... = AUC_p compare_auc_global(fit, X, y, biomarkers = paste0("X", 1:4))  # Single-biomarker DeLong test against chance roc_test(X[, 1], y)

code: # Cross-validated AUC (honest out-of-fold estimates) cv <- cv_aucmat(X, y, n_folds = 5, seed = 42)  # Combine top biomarkers into a panel -- six methods, all honestly # nested-cross-validated top4 <- X[, head(cv$results$biomarker, 4)] panel    <- fit_auc_panel(top4, y, method = "ridge",      n_folds = 5) panel_su <- fit_auc_panel(top4, y, method = "su_liu",     n_folds = 5) panel_uw <- fit_auc_panel(top4, y, method = "unweighted", n_folds = 5)  # Does the panel actually beat its individual components? compare_panel_auc(panel, top4, y) plot_roc_panel(panel, top4, y)  # Sample size for 80% power to detect AUC = 0.70 power_auc_matrix(   target_aucs = 0.70, power = 0.80,   prevalence = 0.3, alpha = 0.05 )

title: aucmat: Matrix-First ROC Analysis
text: examples use simulated data known ground truth. aucmat also ships real biomarker data: plasma Olink proteomics BeatMG (NeuroNEXT NN102), randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled trial rituximab – anti-CD20 monoclonal antibody depletes B cells – myasthenia gravis. data(beatmg_baseline) data(beatmg_ontreatment) provide 730 Olink Inflammation-panel proteins, quality-controlled reshaped subject--protein matrices randomized arm outcome, pre-treatment baseline visit (n = 48) latest well-powered -treatment collection (n = 44). Randomization implies true baseline association proteome treatment arm; screening pre-treatment matrix real-data negative control. Zero 730 proteins reach q < 0.05 baseline. Screening -treatment finds eight, AUC 0.5 – systematically lower rituximab: Proteins significant q < 0.05, rituximab vs. placebo -treatment (Timepoint 3, n = 44). Six eight (FCRL2, CD22, TNFRSF13C/BAFF-R, TNFRSF13B/TACI, CD79B, CD72) canonical B-cell surface B-cell-survival-signaling proteins. Finding exactly markers suppressed blinded, randomized comparison expected pharmacodynamic signature anti-CD20 B-cell depletion, post hoc association fit noise – null baseline screen rules simple technical explanation, since batch plate artifact appear every timepoint rather emerge -treatment. Bootstrap rank-stability analysis (auc_stability(), 1,000 replicates) shows FCRL2, CD22, IL17F, TNFRSF13C top 8 98.2-99.8% resamples, CD79B CD72 markedly less stable (30.8% 15.8%) warrant caution. demonstrates rituximab’s expected pharmacodynamic effect circulating B-cell markers; clinical efficacy result – shipped datasets carry randomized arm , trial’s clinical endpoints. title: aucmat: Matrix-First ROC Analysis
text: Screening throughput. desktop workstation (AMD Ryzen 3.5 GHz, 32 GB RAM, R 4.5.1, Windows 11), aucmat screens 100, 1,000, 10,000 biomarkers (n = 500, DeLong inference) 0.54, 5.02, 59.76 seconds respectively. equivalent per-column pROC loop (pROC::roc() pROC::ci.auc() method = "delong") data requires 0.68, 6.42, estimated 64 seconds. speed advantage comes avoiding repeated outcome encoding column-wise rank computation. small p (< 100), difference negligible; wide matrices (p > 1{,}000), per-column overhead pROC::roc() pROC::ci.auc() becomes meaningful. Simulation calibration. Across 27 simulation conditions (n = 100, 300, 500; prevalence averaged 0.2, 0.3, 0.5; target AUC = 0.65, 0.75, 0.85; 200 replicates ), DeLong 95% confidence interval coverage ranged 0.898 (n = 100, AUC = 0.65) 0.957 (n = 500, AUC = 0.65), converging nominal 0.95 sample size increased. coverage slightly nominal small n extreme AUC values, consistent known finite-sample behavior DeLong variance estimator. Coverage DeLong 95% CI, averaged across 27 conditions (200 reps ).

title: aucmat: Matrix-First ROC Analysis
text: table compares aucmat (v0.2.0) five established R packages ROC analysis biomarker evaluation. Features verified package’s CRAN documentation published literature July 2026. Feature comparison R packages ROC analysis biomarker screening. \texttt{+} = supported; \texttt{-} = supported requires manual implementation. Verified CRAN documentation July 2026. pROC supports unpaired comparisons via \texttt{roc.test(paired=FALSE)}; aucmat requires common subjects comparisons. aucmat uniquely provides. Six capabilities , knowledge, available single R package: () matrix-first screening direction-preserving AUC multiplicity adjustment one call; (ii) non-inferiority equivalence (TOST) tests paired AUC comparisons; (iii) omnibus Wald test three correlated AUCs via joint DeLong covariance matrix; (iv) bootstrap rank-stability analysis top-k selection probabilities pairwise co-selection frequencies; (v) two-stage Hurdle-AUC model zero-inflated data recovers discriminative signal standard AUC fails; (vi) multivariable panel combination honestly nested cross-validation dedicated significance test whether combined panel outperforms individual components. use . single biomarker smoothed ROC curves, pROC remains gold standard, offering binormal density smoothing, partial AUC bootstrap inference, Venkatraman’s permutation test whole-curve comparison (Venkatraman Begg 2000). speed extremely wide matrices (>100k columns), colAUC’s Wilcoxon-based column-wise computation recommended, though provides inference. precision-recall analysis highly imbalanced data, precrec’s C++ backend offers fast accurate PR curves confidence bands multiple test sets. exhaustive search composite scores exactly two biomarkers, dtComb’s library 142 methods covers virtually published linear, non-linear, mathematical, machine-learning combination approaches; aucmat’s fit_auc_panel() targets smaller set statistically transparent combination methods (penalized regression, closed-form AUC-maximizing linear combination, assumption-light unweighted baseline) panels size, always honestly nested-cross-validated, built-test whether combining helped. end--end screening hundreds tens thousands biomarkers full statistical inference, multiplicity adjustment, comparison testing, stability assessment, validation, study planning single coherent workflow, aucmat recommended tool.

title: aucmat: Matrix-First ROC Analysis
text: aucmat provides unified, statistically principled framework biomarker screening binary outcomes R. matrix-first design reduces code burden high-dimensional ROC analysis dozens bespoke scripting steps handful function calls consistent S3 interfaces. Cross-validated screening multivariable panel models address -data optimism – latter via fully nested procedure re-derives every outcome-dependent step, including preprocessing, fold’s training portion alone – power analysis tools support prospective study design. Limitations. current version (0.2.0) restricted binary outcomes primary screening workflow (multiclass available experimentally via aucmat_multiclass()). DeLong variance estimator requires common subjects paired comparisons; unpaired comparisons independent cohorts yet implemented (pROC provides via roc.test(paired=FALSE)). Partial AUC computation implemented internal engine (R/partial-auc.R) yet exposed primary aucmat() interface. package targets -memory dense matrices; sparse -disk backends deferred. DeLong variance estimator shows slightly liberal coverage small sample sizes (coverage \approx 0.90 n = 100 AUC = 0.65), consistent known finite-sample behavior; bootstrap intervals recommended n < 100 per class. Future work. Version 0.2.0 closed honest-nested-cross-validation gap panel models identified previous release added two combination methods (closed-form AUC-maximizing linear combination unweighted baseline) alongside existing penalized-regression panels. development roadmap targets three extensions: version 0.3 extend framework multiclass outcomes one-versus-rest, one-versus-one, Hand-Till estimators; version 0.4 incorporate cumulative/dynamic time-dependent AUCs censored survival outcomes (Heagerty et al. 2000; Blanche et al. 2013); version 0.5 introduce sparse-matrix -disk backends genomics-scale data exceeding available RAM.

title: Introduction to aucmat

title: Introduction to aucmat
text: equal-variance binormal model, biomarker values normally distributed class shared variance: X \mid Y = 0 \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu_0, \sigma^2), \qquad   X \mid Y = 1 \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu_1, \sigma^2) AUC linked standardized mean separation \delta = (\mu_1 - \mu_0)/\sigma: \text{AUC} = \Phi\!\left(\frac{\delta}{\sqrt{2}}\right) target specific AUC, invert: \delta = \sqrt{2} \cdot \Phi^{-1}(\text{AUC}). example, AUC = 0.8 requires \delta \approx 1.19 — positive class mean 1.19 standard deviations negative class mean.

title: Introduction to aucmat
text: scRNA-seq, microbiome, detection-limited assays produce data 60%+ values exactly zero. Standard AUC near 0.5 data binormal assumption breaks. hurdle_auc() uses two-stage model: Zero hurdle: P(X=0 | class) via logistic regression Magnitude: standard AUC nonzero values
code: sim_hur <- simulate_hurdle_auc(   n = 400, prevalence = 0.3,   target_hurdle_aucs = c(0.85, 0.72),   zero_rate_neg = c(0.55, 0.80),   zero_rate_pos = c(0.25, 0.70), seed = 1 ) Xh <- as.matrix(sim_hur$data[, 1:2]) yh <- sim_hur$data$truth  # Standard AUC vs Hurdle AUC fit_std <- aucmat(Xh, yh, ci = "none") fit_hur <- hurdle_auc(Xh, yh)  data.frame(   Biomarker     = paste0("X", 1:2),   Standard_AUC  = round(fit_std$results$auc_strength, 3),   Hurdle_AUC    = round(fit_hur$results$hurdle_auc, 3),   Zero_AUC      = round(fit_hur$results$zero_auc, 3),   Nonzero_AUC   = round(fit_hur$results$nonzero_auc, 3) ) #>   Biomarker Standard_AUC Hurdle_AUC Zero_AUC Nonzero_AUC #> 1        X1        0.861      0.039    0.685       0.998 #> 2        X2        0.549      0.211    0.518       0.974 plot_hurdle_diagnostics(fit_hur)

code: # DeLong CIs with Benjamini-Hochberg adjustment (default) fit_delong <- aucmat(X, y, ci = "delong", adjust = "BH")  # Stratified bootstrap CIs fit_boot   <- aucmat(X, y, ci = "bootstrap", boot_n = 2000, seed = 42)  # No inference (fastest — AUC only, no CIs or p-values) fit_none   <- aucmat(X, y, ci = "none")  # Bonferroni adjustment (stricter than BH) fit_bonf   <- aucmat(X, y, ci = "delong", adjust = "bonferroni")  # Complete-case analysis (remove rows with any NA) fit_comp   <- aucmat(X, y, ci = "delong", na_action = "complete")  # Retain data for downstream plotting without supplying X, y again fit_keep   <- aucmat(X, y, ci = "delong", retain_data = TRUE)

title: Introduction to aucmat
text: compare_auc() tests AUC differences biomarkers measured subjects, supporting three hypothesis types three selection modes.

title: Introduction to aucmat
code: # Mode 1: Reference — every other biomarker vs one reference cmp_ref <- compare_auc(fit, X, y, reference = "X1") print(cmp_ref) #> <aucmat_compare>  3 pairwise comparisons #>  biomarker_a biomarker_b     auc_a     auc_b   auc_diff  std_error   conf_low #>           X1          X2 0.8621524 0.7821333 0.08001905 0.02625104 0.02856795 #>           X1          X3 0.8621524 0.6655238 0.19662857 0.02817393 0.14140869 #>           X1          X4 0.8621524 0.6130095 0.24914286 0.02706024 0.19610576 #>  conf_high      p_value q_value n_common n_pos n_neg  hypothesis margin #>  0.1314701 2.301985e-03      NA      500   150   350 superiority      0 #>  0.2518485 2.970837e-12      NA      500   150   350 superiority      0 #>  0.3021800 3.354582e-20      NA      500   150   350 superiority      0  # Mode 2: Top-N — all pairs among top N (with post-selection warning) cmp_top <- compare_auc(fit, X, y, top_n = 3)  # Mode 3: Named set — all pairs within a specific list cmp_set <- compare_auc(fit, X, y, biomarkers = c("X1", "X2", "X4"))

title: Introduction to aucmat
text: Output columns: biomarker_a, biomarker_b, auc_a, auc_b, auc_diff, std_error, conf_low, conf_high, p_value, q_value, n_common, n_pos, n_neg, hypothesis, margin. equivalence, p_value = max(p_lower, p_upper) (TOST) interval 1 - 2\alpha.
code: # 1. Superiority (default): H0: AUC_a = AUC_b cmp_sup <- compare_auc(fit, X, y, reference = "X1",   hypothesis = "superiority")  # 2. Directional superiority: H0: AUC_a <= AUC_b (a is no better) cmp_dir <- compare_auc(fit, X, y, reference = "X1",   hypothesis = "superiority", alternative = "greater")  # 3. Non-inferiority: H0: AUC_a is worse than AUC_b by at least margin cmp_ni <- compare_auc(fit, X, y, reference = "X1",   hypothesis = "noninferiority", margin = 0.05)  # 4. Equivalence (TOST): H0_lower: delta <= -m, H0_upper: delta >= m cmp_eq <- compare_auc(fit, X, y, reference = "X1",   hypothesis = "equivalence", margin = 0.15)

title: Introduction to aucmat
text: Tests H_0: \text{AUC}_1 = \text{AUC}_2 = \dots = \text{AUC}_p common subject set using joint DeLong covariance matrix Wald statistic. two biomarkers, global Wald statistic equals squared paired-DeLong z statistic (within numerical tolerance). default safety limit 100 biomarkers; raise max_biomarkers.
code: sim_big <- simulate_auc_matrix(n = 500, prevalence = 0.3,   target_aucs = c(0.85, 0.80, 0.75, 0.70, 0.65),   correlation = 0.3, structure = "exchangeable", seed = 3) X_big <- as.matrix(sim_big$data[, 1:5]) y_big <- sim_big$data$truth fit_big <- aucmat(X_big, y_big, ci = "delong", adjust = "BH")  global_test <- compare_auc_global(fit_big, X_big, y_big) print(global_test) #> <aucmat_global_test> #>   H0: all AUCs equal #>   n =500 (150+ / 350-) #>   Wald chi2(4) = 81.859, p = 0.0000 #>  #> AUC estimates: #>     X1     X2     X3     X4     X5  #> 0.8821 0.7907 0.7734 0.6963 0.6946  # Select specific biomarkers compare_auc_global(fit_big, X_big, y_big,   biomarkers = c("X1", "X2", "X3")) #> <aucmat_global_test> #>   H0: all AUCs equal #>   n =500 (150+ / 350-) #>   Wald chi2(2) = 27.251, p = 0.0000 #>  #> AUC estimates: #>     X1     X2     X3  #> 0.8821 0.7907 0.7734

title: Practical Biomarker Screening with aucmat
text: vignette walks realistic biomarker screening analysis using aucmat, simulation reporting. follow typical omics workflow: generate data known properties, screen biomarkers, compare leading candidates, validate via cross-validation, combine panel, assess power follow-study.

title: Practical Biomarker Screening with aucmat
text: simulate 500 subjects (30% cases) 20 biomarkers. first 5 moderate--strong signals (AUC 0.65–0.85); remaining 15 noise (AUC 0.50–0.55). Biomarkers within functional groups correlated.
code: set.seed(42) n_biomarkers <- 20 signal_aucs <- c(0.85, 0.78, 0.72, 0.68, 0.65) noise_aucs <- runif(15, 0.50, 0.55) target_aucs <- c(signal_aucs, noise_aucs)  sim <- simulate_auc_matrix(   n = 500, prevalence = 0.3,   target_aucs = target_aucs,   correlation = 0.15, structure = "exchangeable",   seed = 42 )  X <- as.matrix(sim$data[, 1:20]) y <- sim$data$truth

title: Practical Biomarker Screening with aucmat

title: Practical Biomarker Screening with aucmat
Practical Biomarker Screening with aucmat
text: Now let’s add zero-inflation simulate scRNA-seq-like data compare standard AUC two-stage Hurdle-AUC.  Hurdle-AUC decomposes discrimination zero-inflation signal magnitude signal, recovering AUCs standard screening misses zero-heavy biomarkers.
code: set.seed(123) sim_hur <- simulate_hurdle_auc(   n = 400, prevalence = 0.3,   target_hurdle_aucs = c(0.85, 0.72, 0.60, 0.55, 0.52),   zero_rate_neg = c(0.55, 0.30, 0.70, 0.80, 0.90),   zero_rate_pos = c(0.25, 0.10, 0.60, 0.75, 0.88) ) Xh <- as.matrix(sim_hur$data[, 1:5]) yh <- sim_hur$data$truth  # Standard AUC fit_std <- aucmat(Xh, yh, ci = "none")  # Hurdle AUC fit_hur <- hurdle_auc(Xh, yh) # Side-by-side comparison comp <- data.frame(   Biomarker     = paste0("X", 1:5),   Standard_AUC  = round(fit_std$results$auc_strength, 3),   Hurdle_AUC    = round(fit_hur$results$hurdle_auc, 3),   Zero_AUC      = round(fit_hur$results$zero_auc, 3),   Nonzero_AUC   = round(fit_hur$results$nonzero_auc, 3),   Zero_Rate     = round(fit_hur$results$zero_rate_total, 3) ) comp$Improvement <- round((comp$Hurdle_AUC - comp$Standard_AUC) / comp$Standard_AUC * 100, 1) comp #>   Biomarker Standard_AUC Hurdle_AUC Zero_AUC Nonzero_AUC Zero_Rate Improvement #> 1        X1        0.909      0.166    0.631       0.992     0.450       -81.7 #> 2        X2        0.801      0.377    0.612       0.998     0.265       -52.9 #> 3        X3        0.618      0.287    0.560       0.999     0.675       -53.6 #> 4        X4        0.512      0.900    0.465       0.996     0.767        75.8 #> 5        X5        0.506      0.899    0.497       0.962     0.863        77.7 plot_hurdle_diagnostics(fit_hur)

title: Practical Biomarker Screening with aucmat
text: Combine top biomarkers single score. fit_auc_panel() reports -sample AUC honest, nested cross-validated AUC: penalty selection centering/scaling re-derived fold’s training portion alone, held-fold never leaks model evaluated .
code: # Select top 4 biomarkers by CV AUC top4 <- head(cv$results$biomarker, 4) X_panel <- X[, top4, drop = FALSE]  panel <- fit_auc_panel(X_panel, y, method = "ridge", n_folds = 5, seed = 123) print(panel) #> <aucmat_panel>  ridge #>   AUC (training): 0.9108 #>   AUC (CV):       0.9074 #>   Optimism:       0.0034 #>  #> Coefficients: #>     X1     X2     X3     X5  #> 1.0974 0.7799 0.4516 0.2919 plot(panel)

title: Practical Biomarker Screening with aucmat
text: Ridge one six options. "su_liu" closed-form linear combination maximizes AUC multivariate-normal (binormal) model – fast, glmnet dependency, natural fit alongside package’s binormal simulation engine. "unweighted" sums direction-aligned, standardized biomarkers equal weight fits nothing – useful baseline judging whether fitted methods earning complexity.
code: panel_su <- fit_auc_panel(X_panel, y, method = "su_liu", n_folds = 5, seed = 123) panel_uw <- fit_auc_panel(X_panel, y, method = "unweighted", n_folds = 5, seed = 123)  data.frame(   method   = c("ridge", "su_liu", "unweighted"),   auc_train = c(panel$auc_train, panel_su$auc_train, panel_uw$auc_train),   auc_cv    = c(panel$auc_cv, panel_su$auc_cv, panel_uw$auc_cv) ) #>       method auc_train    auc_cv #> 1      ridge 0.9108000 0.9074095 #> 2     su_liu 0.9103048 0.9015619 #> 3 unweighted 0.8887429 0.8879238

title: Real-Data Application: BeatMG Proteomics
text: Every vignette package uses simulated data known ground truth – useful validating methodology, can’t show aucmat() actually finds real biomarker matrix. vignette , using plasma Olink proteomics BeatMG (NeuroNEXT NN102), randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled trial rituximab myasthenia gravis. Rituximab anti-CD20 monoclonal antibody depletes B cells. gives us real, mechanistically falsifiable question screen : plasma proteome show rituximab’s effect, , proteins? Two datasets ship aucmat: beatmg_baseline: 48 subjects, pre-treatment (Timepoint 1) beatmg_ontreatment: 44 subjects, -treatment (Timepoint 3 – latest collection enough samples AUC inference; Timepoint 4 5) Olink Inflammation + Inflammation_II panels (730 proteins), quality-controlled (?beatmg_baseline exact QC criteria). Randomization means baseline show systematic biomarker–arm association – makes real-data negative control, just second discovery run.

title: Simulating Biomarker Data in aucmat
text: aucmat provides seven simulation functions generating biomarker data controlled properties. vignette explains mathematical foundations, strengths, limitations .

title: Simulating Biomarker Data in aucmat
text: Rather deriving outcome thresholded latent variable, simulate_auc_matrix() fixes outcome Y first draws biomarkers conditional class: X \mid Y = 0 \sim \mathcal{N}_p(\mu_0, \mathbf{R}), \qquad   X \mid Y = 1 \sim \mathcal{N}_p(\mu_1, \mathbf{R}) classes share correlation matrix \mathbf{R}; mean vectors differ. per-biomarker mean shift \delta_k = \mu_{1k} - \mu_{0k} uses closed-form binormal calibration: \delta_k = \sqrt{2} \cdot \Phi^{-1}(\text{AUC}_k) class means centred n-weighted grand mean zero: \mu_0 = \delta \cdot \left(-\frac{n_1}{n}\right), \qquad   \mu_1 = \delta \cdot \left(\frac{n_0}{n}\right) Y fixed biomarker drawn, supplied outcomes keep exact values row order, generated outcomes get exact class counts (default) Bernoulli counts.

title: Simulating Biomarker Data in aucmat
code: set.seed(42)  # 1. Generate outcome from n + prevalence (default: exact class counts) sim_gen <- simulate_auc_matrix(   n = 500, prevalence = 0.3,   target_aucs = c(0.9, 0.8, 0.7, 0.65),   correlation = 0.3, structure = "exchangeable" ) table(sim_gen$data$truth)  # exact 150 / 350 #>  #>   0   1  #> 350 150  # 2. Bernoulli outcome (random class counts) sim_bern <- simulate_auc_matrix(   n = 500, prevalence = 0.3,   target_aucs = c(0.9, 0.8, 0.7, 0.65),   correlation = 0.3, structure = "exchangeable",   outcome_mode = "bernoulli", seed = 45 ) table(sim_bern$data$truth)  # varies around 30% #>  #>   0   1  #> 365 135  # 3. Supplied outcome (exact values preserved) y_supplied <- rbinom(300, 1, 0.3) sim_y <- simulate_auc_matrix(   y = y_supplied,   target_aucs = c(0.85, 0.75),   correlation = 0.3, structure = "exchangeable", seed = 1 ) all(sim_y$data$truth == y_supplied) #> [1] TRUE

title: Simulating Biomarker Data in aucmat
text: every correlation matrix positive definite. feasibility controls response: "error" (default): stops structured condition quantitative diagnostic (eigenvalue, matrix dimensions). "nearest": projects nearest positive-semidefinite matrix via eigenvalue clipping, reports Frobenius adjustment maximum element-wise change.
code: # This is infeasible: exchangeable(0.4) with p=4 is PD; exchangeable(-0.5) is not # because the min eigenvalue of an exchangeable matrix with rho is # (1 - rho) for p-1 multiplicities, needs rho > -1/(p-1) = -1/3 for p=4 sim_adj <- simulate_auc_matrix(   n = 200, prevalence = 0.3,   target_aucs = c(0.8, 0.7, 0.6, 0.55),   correlation = -0.5, structure = "exchangeable",   feasibility = "nearest", seed = 1 ) sim_adj$feasibility #> $status #> [1] "adjusted" #>  #> $adjusted #> [1] TRUE #>  #> $min_eigenvalue #> [1] -0.5 #>  #> $frobenius_adjustment #> [1] 0.5773503 #>  #> $max_abs_adjustment #> [1] 0.1666667

title: Simulating Biomarker Data in aucmat
text: $achieved_correlation computed within-class centred residuals — class centred empirical mean correlating. $requested_correlation (\mathbf{R}) actually parametrizes. raw pooled correlation returned columns (ignoring truth) consistent estimate \mathbf{R} whenever AUC differs 0.5: mixing subpopulations different means inflates pooled correlation (mixture / Simpson’s-paradox effect).

sim_demo <- simulate_auc_matrix(   n = 1000, prevalence = 0.3,   target_aucs = c(0.85, 0.75), correlation = 0.3,   structure = "exchangeable", seed = 1 )  # Naive pooled correlation (wrong estimate of R): cat("Naive pooled cor(X1, X2):             ",     round(cor(sim_demo$data$X1, sim_demo$data$X2), 4), "
") #> Naive pooled cor(X1, X2):              0.4646 # Correct within-class correlation (estimate of R): cat("Within-class centred cor (correct):    ",     round(sim_demo$achieved_correlation[1, 2], 4), "
") #> Within-class centred cor (correct):     0.3279 cat("Requested correlation (R):             ",     sim_demo$requested_correlation[1, 2], "
") #> Requested correlation (R):              0.3

text: Bias small relative auc_mc_se (Monte Carlo standard error). bias consistently much larger MC SE across replicates, simulator systematic calibration error. RMSE captures bias variance. small times, RMSE dominated sampling variation; large times, remaining RMSE reflects bias. Hit rate depends tolerance. tolerance$auc = 0.02, roughly 95% samples hit Monte Carlo SE achieved AUC 0.01 (.e., n around 300-500). Hit rates ~30% times = 100 can indicate calibration problem simply tolerance tight chosen n.

title: Simulating Biomarker Data in aucmat
text: n_failed records replicates simulate_auc_matrix() threw error. Requires least 2 successful replicates. Successful failed counts always reported.

title: Simulating Biomarker Data in aucmat
text: binary outcome Y arises latent continuous variable Y^*: Y^* \sim \mathcal{N}(0, 1), \qquad   Y = \mathbf{1}\{Y^* > \tau\}, \qquad   \tau = \Phi^{-1}(1 - \pi) Biomarkers (X_1, \ldots, X_p, Y^*) jointly multivariate normal: \begin{pmatrix} X_1 \\ \vdots \\ X_p \\ Y^* \end{pmatrix} \sim   \mathcal{N}_{p+1}\left(   \mathbf{0},   \begin{pmatrix}   \mathbf{R} & \boldsymbol{\rho} \\   \boldsymbol{\rho}^\top & 1   \end{pmatrix}   \right) \mathbf{R} p \times p -biomarker correlation matrix \rho_k = \text{Cor}(X_k, Y^*) governs biomarker-outcome relationship.

title: Simulating Biomarker Data in aucmat
text: prevalence \pi, AUC strictly increasing function \rho_k: \text{AUC}(\rho_k, \pi) = f(\rho_k, \pi) f involves bivariate normal CDF (closed form). Calibration uses bisection large sample (n = 100,000 default).

title: Simulating Biomarker Data in aucmat
text: Biomarkers constructed highest lowest AUC: first biomarker X_1 drawn full mean separation \delta_1 = \sqrt{2} \cdot \Phi^{-1}(\text{AUC}_1). k \geq 2, X_k built two parts: Correlated component: linear combination X_1, \ldots, X_{k-1} weighted \mathbf{R}_{k, 1:k-1} \cdot \mathbf{R}_{1:k-1, 1:k-1}^{-1} Residual component: independent normal carrying remaining mean separation needed hit \text{AUC}_k accounting correlated component already contributes X_k centred scaled unit variance.

title: Simulating Biomarker Data in aucmat

title: Simulating Biomarker Data in aucmat
text: constructing ranks target AUC, strictly increasing Box-Cox transform tuned move Pearson correlation toward target without changing AUC (since monotone transforms preserve ranks, hence preserve AUC). every (AUC, correlation) pair achievable — Box-Cox family can move correlation within bounded range [r_{\min}, r_{\max}].
code: out_cor <- generate_auc_cor_vector(   y, target_auc = 0.80, target_cor = 0.45,   shuffle_within_class = FALSE )  c(AUC = out_cor$achieved_auc, Correlation = out_cor$achieved_cor,   lambda = out_cor$lambda,   cor_min = out_cor$cor_range[1], cor_max = out_cor$cor_range[2]) #>         AUC Correlation      lambda     cor_min     cor_max  #>   0.7999774   0.4499979   1.2136369  -0.1520286   0.6017683

title: Simulating Biomarker Data in aucmat
text: copula simulator cleanly separates two orthogonal concerns: Phase 1 — Gaussian Copula: class independently, biomarkers drawn MVN(0, R) rank-transformed uniforms probit-transformed back normal scores. copula step ensures rank correlation structure \mathbf{R} preserved exactly, regardless marginal distributions. Phase 2 — Iterative AUC Perturbation: biomarker whose achieved AUC deviates target, small class-conditional mean shift applied. shift magnitude follows binormal calibration \delta = \sqrt{2} \cdot \Phi^{-1}(\text{AUC}) decays iteration (step size \times step_decay{}^\text{iter}). means shifted (correlations), correlation structure Phase 1 minimally disturbed.

title: Simulating Biomarker Data in aucmat
code: set.seed(42) sim_cop <- simulate_auc_copula(   n = 500, prevalence = 0.3,   target_aucs = c(0.85, 0.75, 0.65),   corr_matrix = matrix(c(     1.0, 0.4, 0.2,     0.4, 1.0, 0.3,     0.2, 0.3, 1.0   ), 3, 3) )  data.frame(   Target   = sim_cop$target_aucs,   Phase1   = round(sim_cop$phase1_aucs, 3),   Achieved = round(sim_cop$achieved_aucs, 3) ) #>    Target Phase1 Achieved #> X1   0.85  0.853    0.853 #> X2   0.75  0.752    0.752 #> X3   0.65  0.651    0.651  cat("\nAchieved correlation:\n") #>  #> Achieved correlation: round(sim_cop$achieved_correlation, 3) #>       X1    X2    X3 #> X1 1.000 0.397 0.216 #> X2 0.397 1.000 0.325 #> X3 0.216 0.325 1.000

title: Simulating Biomarker Data in aucmat
text: scRNA-seq similar assays, large fraction measurements exactly zero. standard binormal model represent : require tail normal distribution zero, producing implausible negative values. hurdle model separates data-generating process two stages: Stage 1 — Zero Hurdle: P(X_j = 0 \mid y) = \text{logistic}(\beta_0 + \beta_1 y) difference zero rates classes provides discriminative signal. Stage 2 — Magnitude (expressed): X_j \mid X_j > 0, y \sim \text{LogNormal}(\mu_y, \sigma^2) \mu_1 - \mu_0 calibrated target non-zero AUC via binormal shift \delta = \sqrt{2} \cdot \Phi^{-1}(\text{AUC}_{\text{nonzero}}).

title: Simulating Biomarker Data in aucmat
code: set.seed(1) sim_hur <- simulate_hurdle_auc(   n = 500, prevalence = 0.3,   target_hurdle_aucs = c(0.85, 0.72, 0.55),   zero_rate_neg = c(0.55, 0.30, 0.80),   zero_rate_pos = c(0.25, 0.10, 0.70) )  # Check achieved zero rates vs targets data.frame(   Biomarker     = paste0("X", 1:3),   Target_ZeroNeg = c(0.55, 0.30, 0.80),   Achieved_ZeroNeg = round(sim_hur$achieved_zero_rate_neg, 3),   Target_ZeroPos  = c(0.25, 0.10, 0.70),   Achieved_ZeroPos = round(sim_hur$achieved_zero_rate_pos, 3) ) #>   Biomarker Target_ZeroNeg Achieved_ZeroNeg Target_ZeroPos Achieved_ZeroPos #> 1        X1           0.55            0.566           0.25            0.193 #> 2        X2           0.30            0.266           0.10            0.100 #> 3        X3           0.80            0.769           0.70            0.633

title: Scalable and Statistically Principled ROC Analysis for High-Dimensional Biomarker Matrices
text: Scalable statistically principled ROC analysis high-dimensional biomarker matrices R. aucmat matrix-first R package univariate screening ranking molecular biomarkers (genes, proteins, metabolites) measured subjects. reports direction-preserving AUCs DeLong stratified-bootstrap inference, multiplicity adjustment, paired comparisons (superiority, non-inferiority, equivalence), omnibus global testing, bootstrap rank-stability analysis, multivariate simulation engine validation.

title: Scalable and Statistically Principled ROC Analysis for High-Dimensional Biomarker Matrices
code: remotes::install_github("vanhungtran/aucmat")

title: Scalable and Statistically Principled ROC Analysis for High-Dimensional Biomarker Matrices
text: aucmat ships real plasma Olink proteomics BeatMG (NeuroNEXT NN102), randomized, placebo-controlled trial rituximab (anti-CD20, B-cell-depleting antibody) myasthenia gravis: Screening pre-treatment matrix finds 0/730 significant proteins (expected null result randomization). Screening -treatment finds 8/730 q < 0.05, systematically lower rituximab — six canonical B-cell surface/signaling markers (FCRL2, CD22, TNFRSF13C/BAFF-R, TNFRSF13B/TACI, CD79B, CD72), expected pharmacodynamic signature anti-CD20 B-cell depletion. See Real-Data Application vignette full analysis, DeLong-vs-bootstrap cross-check, bootstrap rank-stability validation.
code: data(beatmg_baseline)     # pre-treatment, n = 48 data(beatmg_ontreatment)  # on-treatment, n = 44 protein_cols <- setdiff(names(beatmg_ontreatment), c("SampleID", "arm"))  fit <- aucmat(as.matrix(beatmg_ontreatment[, protein_cols]),   beatmg_ontreatment$arm, positive = "Rituximab", ci = "delong", adjust = "BH")

title: Scalable and Statistically Principled ROC Analysis for High-Dimensional Biomarker Matrices
code: library(aucmat)  # 1. Generate correlated biomarkers with controlled AUCs sim <- generate_data_probit(   n = 500, prevalence = 0.3,   target_aucs = c(0.90, 0.80, 0.70),   corr_matrix  = matrix(c(1, 0.3, 0.1, 0.3, 1, 0.2, 0.1, 0.2, 1), 3, 3) )  # 2. Screen all biomarkers fit <- aucmat(sim$data[, 1:3], sim$data$truth) print(fit)  # 3. Visualize plot_auc_rank(fit)       # ordered discrimination strengths plot_auc_volcano(fit)    # effect vs evidence plot_auc_forest(fit)     # multiple AUCs with CIs plot_roc_top(fit, X = sim$data[, 1:3], y = sim$data$truth)  # 4. Compare AUCs — three comparison modes compare_auc(fit, sim$data[, 1:3], sim$data$truth, top_n = 3)          # all pairs compare_auc(fit, sim$data[, 1:3], sim$data$truth, reference = "X1")   # vs reference compare_auc(fit, sim$data[, 1:3], sim$data$truth,   biomarkers = c("X1", "X2"))                                         # named set  # 5. Global test — are all AUCs equal? compare_auc_global(fit, sim$data[, 1:3], sim$data$truth)  # 6. Assess rank stability stab <- auc_stability(sim$data[, 1:3], sim$data$truth, times = 500, seed = 42) plot_auc_stability(stab)

title: Scalable and Statistically Principled ROC Analysis for High-Dimensional Biomarker Matrices
text: aucmat() computes direction-preserving AUCs every biomarker column binary outcome. supports DeLong bootstrap inference, multiplicity adjustment, feature-wise complete-case missing-data handling.
code: fit <- aucmat(X, y)                                       # default: DeLong, BH fit <- aucmat(X, y, ci = "bootstrap", boot_n = 2000)      # bootstrap CIs fit <- aucmat(X, y, ci = "none")                          # no inference (fastest) fit <- aucmat(X, y, adjust = "bonferroni")                # stricter adjustment fit <- aucmat(X, y, na_action = "complete")               # complete-case only fit <- aucmat(X, y, retain_data = TRUE)                   # keep data for plotting

title: Scalable and Statistically Principled ROC Analysis for High-Dimensional Biomarker Matrices
text: compare_auc() tests AUC differences biomarkers measured subjects. supports three selection modes, three hypothesis types, DeLong bootstrap inference.

title: Scalable and Statistically Principled ROC Analysis for High-Dimensional Biomarker Matrices
text: alternative = "two.sided" (default), "greater", "less". Output columns: biomarker_a, biomarker_b, auc_a, auc_b, auc_diff, std_error, conf_low, conf_high, p_value, q_value, n_common, n_pos, n_neg, hypothesis, margin. top_n selects tests data, result carries selection_status = "same_data" warning inference exploratory.
code: # Two-sided superiority (default) compare_auc(fit, X, y, reference = "X1")  # Directional: H0: AUC_a ≤ AUC_b (biomarker a is no better than b) compare_auc(fit, X, y, reference = "X1", alternative = "greater")  # Non-inferiority: H0: AUC_a is worse than AUC_b by at least 0.05 compare_auc(fit, X, y, reference = "X1",   hypothesis = "noninferiority", margin = 0.05)  # Equivalence (TOST): are AUCs within ±0.15? compare_auc(fit, X, y, reference = "X1",   hypothesis = "equivalence", margin = 0.15)

title: Scalable and Statistically Principled ROC Analysis for High-Dimensional Biomarker Matrices
text: compare_auc()’s result plot() method: read : row one pairwise auc_diff (reference minus biomarker) CI. dashed line 0 “difference.” CI doesn’t cross statistically supported difference chosen alternative/hypothesis; one cross means can’t yet distinguish two biomarkers data.
code: cmp <- compare_auc(fit, X, y, reference = "X1") plot(cmp)

title: Scalable and Statistically Principled ROC Analysis for High-Dimensional Biomarker Matrices
code: compare_auc(fit, X, y, top_n = 5, adjust = "BH") compare_auc(fit, X, y, reference = "X1", adjust = "holm") compare_auc(fit, X, y, biomarkers = c("X1","X2","X3"), adjust = "bonferroni")

title: Scalable and Statistically Principled ROC Analysis for High-Dimensional Biomarker Matrices
text: Tests H₀: AUC₁ = AUC₂ = … = AUCₚ common subject set using joint DeLong covariance matrix Wald statistic. Output: χ² statistic, degrees freedom, p-value, per-biomarker AUC estimates, covariance matrix, contrast specification, sample counts. default safety limit 100 biomarkers; raise max_biomarkers. two biomarkers, global Wald statistic equals squared paired-DeLong z statistic (within numerical tolerance).
code: global <- compare_auc_global(fit, X, y)              # all biomarkers global <- compare_auc_global(fit, X, y,   biomarkers = c("X1", "X2", "X3"))                   # selected set print(global)

title: Scalable and Statistically Principled ROC Analysis for High-Dimensional Biomarker Matrices
text: Quantifies much bootstrap-determined ranks change sampling variability. Resamples positive negative subjects separately replacement.
code: stab <- auc_stability(X, y, times = 1000, top_k = c(10, 25, 50), seed = 42) print(stab)              # median rank, Q25/Q75, top-1 frequency, mean/SD AUC head(stab$rank_summary)  # full rank-distribution summary head(stab$top_k_probs)   # top-k selection probabilities stab$coselection          # pairwise co-selection frequencies

title: Scalable and Statistically Principled ROC Analysis for High-Dimensional Biomarker Matrices
text: Standard AUC assumes continuous data following shifted normal distribution per class. breaks zero-inflated data (scRNA-seq, microbiome, detection-limited assays) 60%+ values exactly zero. standard model, zeros come tail N(0,1) 0 — biologically implausible. Hurdle-AUC models zeros expression magnitudes separate biological processes: Stage 1 — Zero expressed? P(X=0 | class) via logistic regression.Stage 2 — expressed, level? Standard AUC nonzero values . read : point one biomarker. x-axis well zero/nonzero pattern discriminates class; y-axis well magnitude, given ’s nonzero discriminates class; point size overall zero rate; colour combined Hurdle AUC. biomarker sitting far right near dashed 0.5 line y-axis (like example’s weaker markers) getting essentially signal whether ’s expressed, much — exactly story standard AUC missed entirely.
code: # scRNA-seq: 62% zeros in controls, 25% in cases sim <- simulate_hurdle_auc(   n = 500, prevalence = 0.3,   target_hurdle_aucs = c(0.85, 0.72, 0.55),   zero_rate_neg = c(0.55, 0.30, 0.80),   zero_rate_pos = c(0.25, 0.10, 0.70) )  # Standard AUC misses the signal fit_std <- aucmat(as.matrix(sim$data[, 1:3]), sim$data$truth, ci = "none") # Hurdle AUC recovers it (+69% improvement) fit_hur <- hurdle_auc(as.matrix(sim$data[, 1:3]), sim$data$truth)  # Diagnostics: zero discrimination vs magnitude discrimination plot_hurdle_diagnostics(fit_hur)

title: Scalable and Statistically Principled ROC Analysis for High-Dimensional Biomarker Matrices
text: Draws biomarkers class-conditionally: X|Y=0 ~ MVN(μ₀, R), X|Y=1 ~ MVN(μ₁, R). outcome Y fixed biomarker drawn — supplied outcomes keep exact values row order; generated outcomes get exact class counts default. Correlation structures: Feasibility diagnostics: requested correlation matrix positive definite, feasibility = "error" (default) stops structured condition. feasibility = "nearest" projects nearest valid matrix reports adjustment magnitude $feasibility.
code: # Exchangeable: all off-diagonal entries equal sim_ex <- simulate_auc_matrix(   n = 500, prevalence = 0.3,   target_aucs = c(0.9, 0.8, 0.7, 0.65),   correlation = 0.4, structure = "exchangeable" )  # AR(1): correlation decays with distance |i−j| sim_ar1 <- simulate_auc_matrix(   n = 500, prevalence = 0.3,   target_aucs = c(0.9, 0.8, 0.7, 0.65),   correlation = 0.6, structure = "ar1" )  # Block: within-block and between-block correlations sim_blk <- simulate_auc_matrix(   n = 500, prevalence = 0.3,   target_aucs = c(0.9, 0.8, 0.7, 0.65),   structure = "block", block_sizes = c(2, 2),   rho_within = 0.6, rho_between = 0.1 )  # User-supplied matrix sim_usr <- simulate_auc_matrix(   n = 500, prevalence = 0.3,   target_aucs = c(0.9, 0.8, 0.7),   correlation = matrix(c(1, 0.3, 0.1, 0.3, 1, 0.2, 0.1, 0.2, 1), 3, 3),   structure = "user" )  # Supplied outcome (retains exact values and row order) y_supplied <- rbinom(500, 1, 0.3) sim_y <- simulate_auc_matrix(   y = y_supplied,   target_aucs = c(0.9, 0.8, 0.7), correlation = 0.3, structure = "exchangeable" )

text: binormal model, biomarker values normally distributed class, shifted apart: AUC determined standardized mean separation δ = (μ₁ − μ₀)/σ: \text{AUC} = \Phi\left(\frac{\delta}{\sqrt{2}}\right) target specific AUC, invert: δ = √2 · Φ⁻¹(AUC). multivariate simulation, biomarkers share correlation matrix R. simulate_auc_matrix() generate_data_analytical() use equal-variance binormal relationship (fast, closed-form). generate_data_probit() uses latent probit model numerical calibration independent AUC-correlation control.

what: What aucmat Does That No Other Package Does
title: Scalable and Statistically Principled ROC Analysis for High-Dimensional Biomarker Matrices
text: End--end workflow: simulate → screen → compare → CV → panel → power one API. Matrix-first: aucmat(X, y) screens columns one call using proper multiplicity. Direction-preserving: reports auc_raw (observed, may <0.5), auc_strength (≥0.5), effect_direction — never silently reverses biomarkers. Non-inferiority & equivalence: TOST-based comparisons user-specified margins. Omnibus global Wald test: tests H0: AUC₁ = … = AUCₚ via joint DeLong covariance. Bootstrap rank-stability: much rankings change sampling? Built-simulator validation: generate correlated biomarkers known truth, confirm simulator calibrated.

title: Fit and validate a multivariable biomarker panel — fit_auc_panel
text: X Numeric matrix (n x p). y Binary outcome. positive Optional positive class label. method "ridge" (default), "lasso", "elasticnet", "logistic" (unpenalized), "su_liu" (closed-form AUC-maximizing linear combination binormal model), "unweighted" (equal-weight sum direction-aligned standardized biomarkers; fitting). alpha Elastic-net mixing: 0 = ridge, 1 = lasso. used method = "elasticnet"; defaults 0.5 (true ridge/lasso blend) supplied. Ignored methods. shrinkage Shrinkage intensity [0, 1] toward diagonal covariance, used method = "su_liu". 0 uses raw pooled covariance (unstable infeasible p approaches n); 1 shrinks way diagonal (independence) covariance. Default 0.1. n_folds Outer CV folds honest AUC (0 = CV, uses training AUC ). Default 5. seed Optional seed CV fold-assignment reproducibility. standardize Center/scale predictors fitting. Default TRUE. Centering scaling always computed relevant training data (full data final model; outer training fold honest CV score), never held-rows.

text: Models binary outcome arising latent continuous variable Y* ~ N(0,1) threshold tau = qnorm(1 - pi). Biomarkers (X_1, ..., X_p, Y*) jointly multivariate normal full $(p+1) \times (p+1)$ correlation matrix $\Sigma$.

text: n Number observations. target_aucs Numeric vector target AUC values, (0, 1). corr_matrix $p \times p$ target correlation matrix biomarkers.  Must symmetric positive definite. prevalence Proportion positive class, (0, 1). n_cal Calibration sample size numerical root-finding latent correlations.  Larger values give precise AUC targeting cost calibration time.  Default 100000. verify Logical.  TRUE (default), empirically verify achieved AUCs correlations.

title: Generate correlated biomarkers with specified AUCs via latent probit model — generate_data_probit
text: list components: data data.frame columns X1, ..., Xp truth (binary outcome, 0/1). achieved_aucs Empirical AUCs biomarker outcome. achieved_correlations Empirical correlation matrix biomarkers. latent_rhos calibrated latent correlations used generation. Sigma full $(p+1) \times (p+1)$ correlation matrix used.

title: Generate correlated biomarkers with specified AUCs via latent probit model — generate_data_probit
text: approach simultaneously controls -biomarker correlations biomarker's AUC outcome, subject positive-definiteness constraint $\Sigma$. Theoretical basis: latent probit model, biomarker X_k binary outcome Y linked rho_k = Cor(X_k, Y*), latent correlation. given prevalence pi, resulting AUC strictly increasing function rho_k. $$AUC(rho_k, pi) = f(rho_k, pi)$$ function f available closed form (involves bivariate normal CDF), calibrate rho_k numerically via bisection large calibration sample. Relationship binormal model: standard binormal model used generate_data_analytical() assumes X_k | Y=0 ~ N(mu_0, sigma^2) X_k | Y=1 ~ N(mu_1, sigma^2) (equal variance).  links AUC Cor(X_k, Y) single parameter delta = (mu_1 - mu_0) / sigma. latent probit model relaxes constraint allowing correlation biomarkers specified independently biomarker-outcome AUC, full (p+1) x (p+1) matrix. Positive definiteness: matrix $\Sigma$ must positive definite.  user-supplied corr_matrix incompatible calibrated latent correlations $\rho_k$, function uses nearest positive-definite matrix (via Higham's algorithm) issues warning.

title: Generate correlated biomarkers with specified AUCs via latent probit model — generate_data_probit
code: # \donttest{ # Three biomarkers with AUCs 0.9, 0.8, 0.7, moderately correlated set.seed(42) sim <- generate_data_probit(   n = 500,   target_aucs = c(0.9, 0.8, 0.7),   corr_matrix = matrix(c(1.0, 0.3, 0.1, 0.3, 1.0, 0.2, 0.1, 0.2, 1.0), 3, 3),   prevalence = 0.3 )  # Compare targets to achieved values data.frame(   Target  = sim$target_aucs,   Achieved = round(sim$achieved_aucs, 4) ) #>   Target Achieved #> 1    0.9   0.9061 #> 2    0.8   0.7775 #> 3    0.7   0.7200 print(round(sim$achieved_correlations, 3)) #>       X1    X2    X3 #> X1 1.000 0.317 0.081 #> X2 0.317 1.000 0.162 #> X3 0.081 0.162 1.000 # }

title: aucmat 0.2.0
text: Two new fit_auc_panel() combination methods: "su_liu", closed-form linear combination maximizes AUC multivariate-normal (binormal) model (Su & Liu, 1993), shrinkage toward diagonal covariance stability biomarkers collinear p approaches n; "unweighted", fitting-free equal-weight sum direction-aligned standardized biomarkers, useful assumption-light baseline New predict.aucmat_panel(): apply fitted panel’s coefficients (stored centering/scaling) new subjects New plot_roc_panel(): ROC curve fitted panel’s honest cross-validated score overlaid top individual biomarkers – non-deprecated, honestly-scored successor plot_roc_with_combos() New compare_panel_auc(): DeLong bootstrap paired test whether combined panel actually outperforms individual biomarkers, reusing compare_auc() engine

title: aucmat 0.2.0
text: Nested cross-validation. reported auc_cv previously reused penalty (lambda.min) selected full dataset inside every outer CV fold, centering/scaling likewise computed full data. now re-derived fold’s training portion , auc_cv longer carries resulting optimistic bias. Elastic-net default. method = "elasticnet" defaulted alpha = 0, ridge regression – elastic net never actually differed ridge unless user manually supplied alpha. default now 0.5. RNG safety. fit_auc_panel(..., seed = ...) previously left caller’s global .Random.seed mutated exit, final (full-data) model’s internal penalty-selection randomness covered seed , results fully reproducible. final fit honest CV loop now run inside single with_seed() block.

title: aucmat 0.2.0
text: fit_auc_panel()’s fitted_model component now list coefficients/means/sds (used predict.aucmat_panel()) instead raw glmnet/glm fit object, methods uniformly. plot_roc_top() plot_roc_panel() now default add_ci = TRUE (bootstrap confidence ribbons shown), matching plot_roc_smooth()’s existing default. Pass add_ci = FALSE faster, ribbon-free plot.

title: aucmat 0.1.0
text: compare_auc() now supports superiority, non-inferiority, equivalence (TOST) hypotheses Configurable alternative (two.sided, greater, less) conf_level DeLong bootstrap methods paired comparisons Post-selection warning top_n used data New compare_auc_global(): omnibus Wald test equality multiple correlated AUCs via joint DeLong covariance Structured condition classes errors RNG safety (with_seed)

title: aucmat 0.1.0
text: New simulate_auc_matrix(): class-conditional multivariate normal biomarker simulator closed-form binormal mean-shift calibration target AUCs Parametrized correlation structures: user (full matrix), exchangeable, ar1, block Feasibility diagnostics error/nearest handling nearest positive-definite projection infeasible correlation targets New validate_simulation(): repeated-draw calibration harness reporting bias, RMSE, Monte Carlo standard error, target-interval hit rates AUCs pairwise correlations
